metadata.title: Claim the Name: URL Availability + Final Pick
metadata.playlist: Name It & Brand It — Your Strategic Identity
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 6 brief — Brand Strategy + Naming

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 5 of 7. You have three names that passed the trademark screen. One more gate: URL availability. Check dot-com, dot-ai, dot-io, and dot-app for each of the three. Then pick the final name based on trademark clearance and URL together. One important note before we go further: checking whether a domain is available is free research. Actually buying the domain is a real-money decision — the same instinct as stopping before you hit purchase on anything else in this course. Check availability now, buy deliberately later. Let's go.

props.segment: Claim the Name: URL Availability + Final Pick
props.command: 3 names → check .com / .ai / .io / .app → assess + pick final name. Checking = free. Buying = real-money decision.
props.runningText: availability check is free research — buying is a deliberate purchase, not an impulse…
output: Final name recommendation with trademark + URL justification
Alternate TLD or spelling options if .com is taken

beats.narration_text: The four TLDs to check are dot-com, dot-ai, dot-io, and dot-app. Why these four? Dot-com is the default trust signal — audiences default to it, and if someone else owns your name dot-com, they may receive traffic intended for you. Dot-ai signals AI-related tools specifically — if your Madison tool is an AI pipeline, dot-ai is a legitimate first choice and is increasingly recognized. Dot-io is common in technical tools and developer products — strong signal in that community. Dot-app is a newer Google-managed TLD that reads as polished and credible for software products. To check availability, go to a domain registrar — namecheap.com, domains.google, or any major registrar — and type each name. The registrar will show you which extensions are available and their price. You are doing research, not purchasing. Take a screenshot of the availability results for each name. Document the results in a table: three rows for the three names, four columns for the TLDs, and in each cell note available or taken. If a preferred TLD is taken, note who holds it — a parked domain is different from an active site.

artifactLines: .com: default trust signal; if someone owns it, they may get your traffic.
.ai: strong signal for AI tools — legitimate first-choice for a Madison pipeline.
.io: recognized in technical/developer communities.
.app: Google-managed; polished signal for software products.
Document: 3-name × 4-TLD table. Note 'parked' vs 'active site' if taken.
props.sparkLine: Checking availability = free research. Write it down. Buy it later.

beats.act: alternate spellings and the real-money aside
beats.narration_text: If all four TLDs are taken for your preferred name, you have two options before you give up on the name. First, consider alternate spellings — a dropped vowel, a numeral, a hyphen. 'SourceTrace' with dot-com taken might be available as 'SrcTrace' or 'SourceTracer' or 'SourceTrace.ai.' A slight variation that preserves the sound and meaning can free up the name without losing the brand equity. Second, consider whether a less popular TLD is acceptable for the project's scope. For a class assignment portfolio, dot-io or dot-ai is entirely credible. Document whatever you find — availability, alternate spellings you considered, and the option you recommend. Then the real-money aside. When a registrar shows you an available domain, the screen will typically have a large button that says 'Add to Cart' or 'Get' or 'Register.' Do not click it during this research session. Write down the name and the TLD that is available. The actual purchase is a separate decision — made deliberately, not in the middle of a research session where the button is right in front of you.

artifactLines: Option 1: alternate spelling — dropped vowel, suffix, slight variation that preserves sound + meaning.
Option 2: accept a less-common TLD — .io or .ai is credible for a portfolio tool.
Document: what you found · alternatives you considered · what you recommend.
REAL-MONEY ASIDE: 'Add to Cart' button is right there. Do not click it during research.
The purchase is a separate deliberate decision — not an impulse during a research session.
props.sparkLine: Write down what's available. Buy it deliberately, not in the moment.

beats.narration_text: Now pick the final name. The decision is based on two things together: trademark clearance from Episode 4 and URL availability from this episode. A name that passed the trademark screen but has no available URL in any of the four TLDs is a harder choice. A name with low trademark risk and an available dot-ai or dot-com is a strong candidate. The final recommendation has three parts. First: the name you chose. Second: the TLD that is available. Third: a justification in two to three sentences that names both the trademark outcome and the URL outcome — for example, 'Meridian.ai — Justia returned zero live results in Classes 9 or 42, and meridian.ai is available as of the date of this search. This name also fits the Personality pillar from Episode 2, which described the tool as precise and directional.' That third part is where Episodes 1 through 5 connect — the final name has to make sense given the brand foundation you built. A name chosen purely on availability, with no connection to the pillars or positioning, is a name that fights the brand. The connection should be explicit.

artifactLines: Decision: trademark clearance (E4) + URL availability (this episode) combined.
Final recommendation: name · TLD available · 2–3 sentence justification.
Justification must name: trademark outcome + URL outcome + brand alignment.
Brand alignment = connects to pillars and positioning from Episodes 1–2.
A name chosen purely on availability that fights the brand is the wrong name.
props.sparkLine: Trademark + URL + brand fit. All three. That's the final name.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for URL Availability and Final Name. Check dot-com, dot-ai, dot-io, and dot-app for the three names from Episode 4. Document results in a table — available or taken, and whether a taken domain is parked or active. If all four TLDs are taken, consider an alternate spelling that preserves the sound and meaning. Do not purchase anything during the research session — checking is free, buying is a deliberate separate decision. Pick the final name based on trademark clearance and URL availability together. Write a two-to-three sentence justification that names both outcomes and ties the name to the brand pillars. Episode 6 is portfolio analysis.

props.artifactTitle: Claim the Name: URL Availability + Final Pick
artifactLines: Check .com / .ai / .io / .app for all 3 names. Document: available or taken (parked vs active).
All 4 taken → alternate spelling that preserves sound + meaning.
Do not purchase during research — write down availability, buy deliberately later.
Final name: trademark clearance + URL availability + brand alignment → 2–3 sentence justification.
Name that fights the pillars = wrong name, even if the URL is free.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. After you have run the URL checks and taken your screenshots, use this prompt to draft the final name recommendation. Paste it into Claude and fill in the brackets. The prompt reads: I have checked URL availability for three names. Here are the results. Name 1: [name]. Available TLDs from .com/.ai/.io/.app: [list available, list taken]. Note if any taken TLD has an active site versus a parked domain: [describe]. Name 2 and Name 3 in the same format. From Episode 4, the trademark risk ratings were: Name 1: [risk]. Name 2: [risk]. Name 3: [risk]. From my brand foundation in Episodes 1 and 2, the key Personality traits are [list them] and the Differentiation pillar is [paste it]. Draft a final name recommendation that: names the chosen name and TLD, states the trademark outcome and URL availability as two separate facts, and explains in one to two sentences how the name fits the brand Personality traits and Differentiation pillar. Also note any alternate TLD or spelling options I should keep in reserve. After you get the draft, read it out loud — if the connection between the name and the brand feels forced, rewrite the justification in your own words until it sounds natural.

props.segment: Claim the Name: URL Availability + Final Pick
props.command: 3 names. URL results: [available/taken per TLD]. Trademark risks from E4: [H/M/L each]. Brand personality traits: [list]. Differentiation pillar: [paste]. Draft: chosen name + TLD · trademark outcome · URL outcome · brand fit (1–2 sentences). Note reserve TLDs/spellings.
props.runningText: if the brand-fit justification sounds forced, rewrite it in your own words until it sounds natural…

beats.narration_text: Claim the Name: URL Availability + Final Pick. Liam, in for Bear.
